data_in.description: bioRxiv preprint 739011 (Huidong Chen et al.) — clustering benchmark results table or supplementary file

data_out.description: Structured TSV table with methods as rows, datasets as columns, and clustering accuracy metrics (NMI, ARI, or purity) as cell values
Summary statistics (mean, median accuracy per method; per-dataset ranking) demonstrating SnapATAC superiority and chromVAR kmers+PCA as best chromVAR variant

domain_knowledge: Clustering quality in chromatin accessibility studies is typically measured by normalized mutual information (NMI), adjusted Rand index (ARI), or purity; higher values indicate better separation of known cell/sample types.
chromVAR kmers+PCA is a specific configuration combining k-mer annotations with principal component analysis dimensionality reduction, distinct from chromVAR with motif-based annotations.

SnapATAC is a competing single-cell ATAC-seq analysis method that uses a different algorithmic approach (approximate nearest neighbor graphs) and is documented to outperform chromVAR on clustering benchmarks.

Benchmark tables in bioinformatics preprints typically report metrics across multiple datasets (cell types, organisms, or peak-calling strategies) to demonstrate generalization; missing datasets in the table must be noted.
The bioRxiv accession 739011 is a publicly deposited preprint version; the benchmark results may differ from a later peer-reviewed publication if one exists.

direct_checks: verify that a TSV table file exists in bioRxiv preprint 739011 (Chen et al.) or its supplementary materials containing clustering accuracy metrics
file_format_is TSV with columns for method name, dataset identifier, and clustering accuracy metric (e.g., ARI, NMI, or purity score)
verify that the table row_count_equals at least 3 for chromVAR variants (including kmers+PCA variant) and at least 1 row for SnapATAC, across at least 2 datasets
value_in_range: clustering accuracy scores are between 0 and 1 (or 0–100 if percentage-scaled); no canonical answer for absolute thresholds since multiple accuracy metrics exist
verify that SnapATAC row values are numerically greater than or equal to baseline chromVAR rows for the same dataset (robust to metric choice if direction is consistent)
verify that chromVAR kmers+PCA variant row has the highest or tied-highest accuracy among all chromVAR variants for each dataset, parameter-sensitive to which datasets are included
expert_review: confirm that the retrieved table matches the summary statement 'SnapATAC outperforms chromVAR for clustering' by inspecting whether SnapATAC values exceed chromVAR baseline across all reported datasets

confirm that the retrieved table supports 'kmers+PCA is the best chromVAR variant' by verifying that kmers+PCA consistently achieves equal or superior clustering accuracy versus other chromVAR approaches (e.g., plain chromVAR, chromVAR+other feature engineering)

assess whether the table structure and metric definitions are consistent with standard clustering evaluation practice (e.g., higher values indicate better clustering, metric is invariant to label permutation)

evidence_snippets.text: [intro] How does SnapATAC's clustering performance compare to chromVAR variants (especially kmers+PCA) according to the Huidong Chen et al. benchmark study?: 'For a paper evaluating chromVAR and other methods as a method for enabling clustering of single cells, see [the preprint from Huidong Chen et al](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/739011v1).'

[intro] SnapATAC outperforms chromVAR for clustering tasks evaluated in the Huidong Chen et al. paper, while kmers+PCA is the best chromVAR variant for clustering.: 'Using kmers + PCA appears to be the best variant of chromVAR for clustering, but newer methods such as SnapATAC outperform chromVAR for the clustering tasks evaluated in the paper.'

[intro] SnapATAC: 'newer methods such as SnapATAC outperform chromVAR for the clustering tasks'

cmd:
curl -s https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/739011v1 -o preprint.html && python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd
import sys

# Read the input benchmark file
input_file = '{inputs.preprint_benchmark_file}'

try:
    # Try to read as CSV/TSV
    df = pd.read_csv(input_file, sep=None, engine='python')
except:
    try:
        # Try as Excel
        df = pd.read_excel(input_file)
    except:
        print('Error: Could not parse input file', file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

# Extract methods and datasets
methods = df.iloc[:, 0].values if len(df) > 0 else []
datasets = df.columns[1:] if len(df.columns) > 1 else []

# Build output matrix
output_df = df.copy()
output_df.to_csv('clustering_benchmark_summary.tsv', sep='\t', index=False)

# Calculate summary statistics
if len(df.columns) > 1:
    numeric_cols = df.iloc[:, 1:].apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
    stats = pd.DataFrame({
        'Method': methods,
        'Mean_Accuracy': numeric_cols.mean(axis=1),
        'Median_Accuracy': numeric_cols.median(axis=1)
    })
    stats.to_csv('clustering_summary_statistics.tsv', sep='\t', index=False)
    print('Summary statistics written to clustering_summary_statistics.tsv')

print('Clustering benchmark table written to clustering_benchmark_summary.tsv')
EOF

workflow_description: 1. Access the bioRxiv preprint 739011 (Chen et al.) and locate the clustering benchmark results table or supplementary data. 2. Extract clustering accuracy metrics (e.g., NMI, ARI, or purity scores) for chromVAR kmers+PCA and SnapATAC variants across all reported datasets. 3. Identify the best-performing chromVAR variant (kmers+PCA) and document relative performance versus SnapATAC baseline. 4. Compile method-by-dataset matrix into a TSV table with rows as methods, columns as datasets, and cells as accuracy scores. 5. Calculate summary statistics (mean, median, rank) for each method across datasets to quantify the finding that SnapATAC outperforms chromVAR overall while chromVAR kmers+PCA is superior to other chromVAR configurations.
